bibliographic_data.title: Search for transient variations of the fine structure constant and dark matter using fiber-linked optical atomic clocks
bibliographic_data.abstract: We search for transient variations of the fine structure constant using data from a European network of fiber-linked optical atomic clocks. By searching for coherent variations in the recorded clock frequency comparisons across the network, we significantly improve the constraints on transient variations of the fine structure constant. For example, we constrain the variation to |δα/α| < 5 × 10−17 for transients of duration 103 s. This analysis also presents a possibility to search for dark matter, the mysterious substance hypothesized to explain galaxy dynamics and other astrophysical phenomena that is thought to dominate the matter density of the universe. At the current sensitivity level, we find no evidence for dark matter in the form of topological defects (or, more generally, any macroscopic objects), and we thus place constraints on certain potential couplings between the dark matter and standard model particles, substantially improving upon the existing constraints, particularly for large (≳104 km) objects.

core_content.novelty_claim: The study significantly improves constraints on transient variations of the fine structure constant using a European network of fiber-linked optical atomic clocks and also explores the possibility of detecting dark matter through these clocks.
core_content.key_methodology: Data from a European network of fiber-linked optical atomic clocks was used to search for coherent variations in clock frequency comparisons. The analysis focused on transient variations of the fine structure constant, with constraints placed at |δα/α| < 5 × 10−17 for transients of duration 103 s.
outcomes: Improved constraints on transient variations of the fine structure constant
No evidence found for dark matter in the form of topological defects or macroscopic objects
core_content.full_text_markdown: The study aimed to search for transient variations of the fine structure constant using a network of fiber-linked optical atomic clocks. By analyzing coherent variations in clock frequency comparisons, significant improvements were made in constraining these transient variations. For example, it was found that |δα/α| < 5 × 10−17 for transients lasting around 103 seconds. Additionally, the analysis explored the possibility of detecting dark matter through these clocks. At current sensitivity levels, no evidence was found for dark matter in the form of topological defects or macroscopic objects, and constraints were placed on potential couplings between dark matter and standard model particles, particularly for large (≳104 km) objects.
